author: @skanderchida9174
text: The idea is that if you spin a motor you get electricity. Now how do you spin a motor? You can use a magnet motor that generates enough torque to spin that motor.. In the specific case of the Liberty  engine 2.0, the magnet motor consists of a combination flywheel plus N52 neodymium magnets. The flywheel is built using a siver alluminum alloy. The reason behind such an alloy, is to have a diamagnetic (repulses magnetism) and relatively dense material that's denser than alluminum alone yet still machineable (not to exceed 25% silver and 75% alluminum, otherwise the alloy becomes brittle and hard to machine).. When you prime the flywheel, using the recoil starter, the combination flywheel, magnets, and microwave transformers with their respective iron core, becomes a pulse motor that spins the 220 Volt motor which in turn energizes those coils. The result is raw energy, that needs to be fed through a 20 kw inverter to get an output with a clean sine wave, otherwise you will fry your electronics.😢

replies.author: @skanderchida9174
replies.text: One more thing, magnets don't go forever, so instead of using epoxy resin use silver alluminum screws instead (avoid ferromagnetic screws).This way when the time comes, you can either replace them with new ones, or you can recycle the old ones by realigning them back to new..

author: @chaorrottai
text:
There's only one problem. Heat pumps. Heat pumps create energy differentials against positive slopes with coefficients of performance greater than 1, IOW an efficiency exceeding 100%.

It's a terrible excuse that the heat already existed and is just being moved, because the same is true of a water pump, except a water pump can't move more kinetic energies worth of water than it took to run the pump.

Heat pumps relocate more heat energy than the amount of energy it took to run the pump AGAINST A POSITIVE SLOPE. That proves it can be done for heat. If it can be done for heat, it will eventually be done for every single other form of energy, it's just a matter of figuring out how.

To further illustrate this: I asked chatGPT
"Is it true that in an electrostatic induction generator like a toepler-holtz machine, that the electrostatic force balance is such that force required to spin the rotor during is almost completely to the output current and voltage of the generator"

It replied:
"In an electrostatic induction generator like a Toepler-Holtz machine, the force required to spin the rotor is not directly related to the output current and voltage of the generator.

The Toepler-Holtz machine operates based on the principle of electrostatic induction, which involves the transfer of electric charge from one object to another without direct contact. In this type of generator, a high voltage is applied to a rotating disk, which is coated with conducting material. The disk is then brought into close proximity to a stationary metal comb, which is also charged with a high voltage.

As the disk rotates, the conducting material on its surface passes under the charged comb, inducing an electric charge on the disk. The charge on the disk is then collected by metal brushes, which are connected to an external circuit. This process continues as the disk rotates, generating an output voltage and current.

The force required to spin the rotor is related to the mechanical friction and other factors, such as air resistance, that oppose the motion of the rotor. The output voltage and current of the generator depend on factors such as the speed of rotation, the voltage applied to the disk, the distance between the disk and the comb, and the geometry of the generator.

Therefore, while the force required to spin the rotor does affect the efficiency of the generator, it is not directly related to the output current and voltage of the generator."

Then I asked:
"I heard that the electrostatic force balance is such that there is a virtually worthless assistive push/pull generated by the electrostatic force balance, is that true?"

and it said:
"It is true that in an electrostatic generator like the Toepler-Holtz machine, the electrostatic force balance does not provide a significant assistive push/pull to help spin the rotor.

The electrostatic force balance is the force that acts on the charged particles in the generator, including the rotor and the stationary comb, due to the interaction of their charges. This force is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

While the electrostatic force balance plays a crucial role in the operation of the generator by inducing charges on the rotor and generating an output voltage and current, it does not provide a significant mechanical force to assist with the rotation of the rotor.

The force required to spin the rotor comes mainly from external sources such as the motor that drives the rotor or from a hand crank used to manually rotate the rotor. Any assistive push/pull generated by the electrostatic force balance is negligible in comparison.

Therefore, while the electrostatic force balance is an essential aspect of the operation of the generator, it does not provide a significant assistive force to help spin the rotor."

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: Converters and motors also have losses, usually felt as heat.  In our imagination, we can see the converter moved by a flywheel so we can have the flywheel add to the potential ' carrots ' in the system.  We can also imagine buckets ( capacitors ) collecting them and timed by an electronic circuit to turn on a motor to keep the flywheel at a set range of rotations per second.  The motor will use the bucket of carrots at small amounts from the buckets, put them through the flywheel to the converter and back into the buckets.  We still will ' run out of carrots ' as we don't have it working a garden to grow more.  One of the potential ' cheats ' is if there is an RF receiver ( think how your tap to pay card works ) powering the electronics.  Add a voltage multiplier to have the ' converter ' be spun by an electrostatic engine.  The math still shows we will run out of ' carrots '.

author: @chrislau9921
text:
Hi Rob, thank you for your video. Greatly put. 

However, we should never forget that everything we consider commonplace and even easy today was just as silly and impossible for people in the past. I believe that as long as we do not know everything about nature and physics (which is never), we should not rule out things and ideas as impossible. Our latest interpretation of the universe is that it emerged out of nothing in the Big Bang. I don't understand why everyone finds it so silly that energy could also emerge from nothing. We simply don't know what or how to search for it yet! And as long as that's the case, I completely agree with you that devices like the Liberty Machine, free energy... only frustrate, mislead and take people down the wrong path.

I thank you for your refreshing way of imparting knowledge and look forward to your videos every day!
Keep up the good work!

author: @ronniepirtlejr2606
text:
Moving carrots!
What ( carrot) moves a witching rod when over a water source?
Or what moves a witching Fork made from a branch when over a water pipe buried several feet under the ground?
It really does work Robert! 
  I used to play with these for hours at a time for years,  when I lived in the Mojave Desert in California as a teenager.
 I remembered the branch having immense strength that could pull the branch down to the ground once over the water pipe!
One time a neighbor kid came up to me and said I was faking it. I told him I guarantee you that you will not be strong enough to hold this Branch up past this line that I draw on the ground!
I told him to pull the fork apart about knee high off the ground.  Walk slowly to that line that I drew on the ground.
See if he was strong enough to pass that line?
When he made it to the line,  the Forked Branch pulled straight down to the ground. He looked at me with his eyes wide open. He took off running home and never spoke to me again!
Like I did some magic thing or something lol.
It wasn't me, it really works Robert!

replies.author: @Nikosi9
replies.text:
Ronnie Pirtle Jr > When my son was about 4 years old, I showed him how to dowse for water. I made a piano wire "rod" for him and he enjoyed finding pipes underground. One day, he said, " I don't need that wire, Dad. I just concentrate on the feeling in my hands, and when there's water around, my hand tingles."
There's a lot of mythology in this practice. I've read where the rod has to be from a willow tree, or have some other special characteristic in order to be effective. It's not true. It's the dowser who sensitizes himself to the presence of the water.

replies.text:
@Nikosi9 interesting!
 Clothes hangers bent in the shape of an L work great for finding water also.
I have also heard that it is the person but, I'm not a 100% certain?

Here's an interesting thing you might like?
I was visiting the cemetery here in Herrin Illinois. 
The cemetery worker was using some blueprints to locate the area of graves when uncertain. 
Once he locates the general area, he pulled out two dowsing rods and moved then over the area.
I asked him what was he doing?
He told me that he could find the exact location of the body under the ground using the rods.

author: @kreynolds1123
text: Also regarding electricity,  many people conflate voltage power and energy.  Even when looking at just voltage, anything other than pure DC is a wave form that will have peek voltage that differ from RMS voltage.  Then if we look at power, there's power factor, apparent power, reactive power and true power.  Unfortunately, not understanding these concepts and and what various test equipment and procedures are actually testing can lead to misunderstanding, false statements of fact, and bad conclusions.
